text: The surgeon performs a robotic sigmoid resection for sigmoid cancer, encountering inflammatory adhesions of the omentum to the tumor. The medial to lateral dissection is carried out along the back of the mesorectum, with care taken to protect the nerves. The ureter is identified and swept down. A lymph node near the IMA aortic junction is resected as a separate specimen. The surgeon clears the IMA distal to its aortic takeoff and proximal to the left colic artery.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, revealing the gonadal vessels. A sponge is placed over the ureter for protection. The sigmoid mesentery is separated from the sidewall with ease, without the need to mobilize the splenic flexure.

The surgeon clears off the rectosigmoid mesentery, ensuring good length for the procedure. The proximal mesentery is taken, and an intracorporeal sewn anastomosis is performed. The specimen is then exteriorized through a fan and steel incision.

The surgeon performs proximal and distal division, aligning the anastomosis by sewing the posterior outer row with a V-lock suture. An otomy is made in the well-vascularized rectum. The intervening segment of rectum between the staple line and the otomy appears ischemic, with a discrepancy in ICG enhancement. The surgeon amputates the staple line and converts to an end-to-end anastomosis with healthy, perfused bowel.

The surgeon lines up the end-to-end anastomosis, ensuring improved perfusion compared to previous attempts. A single layer end-to-end anastomosis is sewn, starting with a trio silk seromuscular bite posteriorly in the midline to maintain alignment. The surgeon sews out to the left side, observing a healthy bowel with a wide open lumen and zero tension, indicating a secure anastomosis.

And now we'll switch to a canal to come over the top. I used to be exclusively a two layer sewn anastomosis surgeon. I have just in the last probably year adopted single layer anastomosis and I have to say I'm very happy with it. Reasons why I don't do this more often is particularly when I have stapled ends of the colon. I just don't necessarily like taking the time to cut the staple lines off. It kind of feels like a waste of the stapler, but in a cancer operation, I do prefer to staple the segment of a specimen so that there's theoretically no tumor shedding during bowel manipulation if I had left that segment with open ends. There are dozens of very appropriate and safe ways to do this same operation.

This is just currently the way that I prefer to do the operation, and I encourage all of us to just do what they are most comfortable with. Again, the teaching point for myself is despite your experience level, despite your skill set, I think that at any point of an operation, if it doesn't look absolutely perfect and you have an opportunity to fix it, I encourage you to do that before you leave the operating room, particularly in anastomosis. It pains me to know that in situations like this, or maybe even if there was an air leak on testing, that some patients might end up getting a diverting stoma. the solution to the problem. And I just think that if you have room to revise your anastomosis, even if it takes you that extra half an hour or an hour, that is what is best for the patient.

The surgeon cannels over the top with a suture sewn to the patient's right, bringing the sutures towards the middle to imbricate the mucosa, ensuring serosa touches serosa. The surgeon goes back and forth to avoid bowing or folding, adding additional silk sutures if necessary. The lip is dunked to ensure complete imbrication, and the ends are tied together. A leak test is performed with irrigant placed posteriorly to release trapped air bubbles, confirming no tension and a secure anastomosis.
